risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Subjects were adult cigarette smokers randomly switched to a tobacco-heating cigarette ... snus ... or an ultra-low machine yield tobacco-burning cigarette ..." Comment: The article states that participants were "randomly switched" and later refers to a "randomized, multi-center study," which suggests allocation was intended to be random, but it does not describe the actual sequence generation method (e.g., computer-generated list, random numbers, blocks, stratification). Under Cochrane guidance, "randomized" alone without further context is insufficient to confirm a truly random sequence, so this remains unclear.

_evidence.method_quote: Subjects were adult cigarette smokers randomly switched to a tobacco-heating cigarette (Eclipse, non-menthol or menthol variety depending on subject preference), snus (Camel SNUS, subject choice of Frost, Spice, and Mellow varieties) or an ultra-low machine yield tobacco-burning cigarette (5 mg Cambridge Filter Method “tar”; Camel or Salem, non-menthol and menthol, respectively, depending on subject preference). A fourth group of never smokers was included for baseline comparisons.
_evidence.method_summary: Subjects were described as randomly switched to one of three product groups; the specific sequence generation method is not described.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The article states that subjects were "randomly switched" to the study products, but it does not describe any method used to conceal the allocation sequence from the person enrolling participants. The extracted evidence notes "mechanism: not described" and "The text describes that subjects were randomized but does not describe how the allocation sequence was concealed from recruiters/assigners." Comment: Randomization alone is not enough to judge allocation concealment low risk; there is no explicit central allocation, SNOSE, identical containers, or other concealment mechanism described, and no positive evidence of an open or predictable schedule either.

_evidence.note: The text describes that subjects were randomized but does not describe how the allocation sequence was concealed from recruiters/assigners.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The article describes a randomized switch to different tobacco products and reports extensive biomarker outcomes, but it does not state whether participants or the personnel delivering the intervention were blinded, nor does it describe any blinding method. Comment: Because blinding status is not specified, and the extracted evidence marks participants/personnel blinding as unclear, the correct Cochrane judgement for performance bias is Unclear risk. Although some outcomes are objective biomarkers, a few outcomes (e.g. mouth level exposure, yield-in-use nicotine) could be influenced by awareness of assignment, so Low risk cannot be assumed from the available information.

Summary: The outcomes were assessed by automated laboratory/measurement systems rather than by a subjective human outcome assessor. The article states that urine and blood samples were "shipped in batch to the appropriate analytical laboratory for analysis," and for COHb that "Absorbancies at 6 different wavelengths were measured and used by the oximeter to generate total hemoglobin and percent COHb." Comment: These are objective biomarkers and machine-based measurements, so knowledge of allocation is unlikely to influence outcome measurement. No subjective outcomes were included in the reported outcome set relevant here, so the worst-outcome rule does not elevate risk.

outcomes.label: mouth level exposure (char line, MLE tar, MLE nicotine, YIU)

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The per-protocol sample was used for change-from-baseline analyses and product group comparisons at weeks 12 and 24, and was defined by mean cumulative compliance greater than 50% ... Sample sizes for the per-protocol sample were: 33 switched to tobacco-heating cigarettes, 20 switched to snus and 35 switched to ultra-low machine yield tobacco-burning cigarettes ... Dropouts were not replaced and missing data were not imputed." Comment: The analysed outcome data at weeks 12 and 24 were restricted to a per-protocol subset rather than all randomized participants, with substantial post-randomization loss/exclusion from analysis (44->33, 43->20, 44->35) and marked imbalance across arms (23 lost in snus vs 9-11 in the other arms; >10 percentage-point differential). No imputation or other robustness analysis is reported, so attrition could bias the results.

per_arm_flow.reasons: per-protocol sample was defined by mean cumulative compliance greater than 50% over the 24 study weeks
per-protocol sample was defined by mean cumulative compliance greater than 50% over the 24 study weeks
per-protocol sample was defined by mean cumulative compliance greater than 50% over the 24 study weeks

_evidence.missing_data_method: per-protocol; dropouts were not replaced and missing data were not imputed
_evidence.notes: Per-arm numbers are from the per-protocol sample used for change-from-baseline analyses and product group comparisons at weeks 12 and 24, not a CONSORT attrition table. Loss computed from randomized vs per-protocol sample sizes.
